summary: Paul Graham argues that successful startups come from three doable fundamentals: start with strong people, build something customers genuinely want, and spend as little as possible. Ideas matter less than execution and adaptability; the real edge is understanding users and iterating quickly. If you can do those, getting rich via a startup is hard but achievable, without any magical brilliance.
key_points: The initial idea is a starting point; good people can fix bad ideas, but bad people can’t save good ideas.
Make something customers want by watching users and iterating fast; prototypes reveal wrong assumptions.
Startups should be technical at the core; business skills are learnable and secondary to product insight.
Niche and low-end markets are often the best entry points; better tech displaces incumbents over time.
Spending slowly and hiring sparingly preserves flexibility and time to learn what the business really is.
money_quotes: You need three things to create a successful startup: to start with good people, to make something customers actually want, and to spend as little money as possible.
Good people can fix bad ideas, but good ideas can't save bad people.
The only way to make something customers want is to get a prototype in front of them and refine it based on their reactions.
Design your product to please users first, and then think about how to make money from it.
If you want to do it, do it.  Starting a startup is not the great mystery it seems from outside.
key_insight: A startup succeeds not through a brilliant idea but through strong founders who understand users, iterate quickly, and conserve cash long enough to discover what people truly want.
